title: Nomewaimea kupenuia Austin & Rubinoff 2026
author: Austin, Kyhl A.
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-zero
description: Nomewaimea kupenuia  Austin & Rubinoff, sp. nov.   http://zoobank.org/ urn:lsid:zoobank.org:act: B04BF691-AB01-4745-8143-93F3429AFCB6  Figs 4G, H; 6B   Diagnosis.   Nomewaimea kupenuia  is unlikely to be confused with any other species of Tortricidae in Hawaiʻi. Its distinctive bicolored FW, with golden yellow basal third and mahogany red distal two-thirds should serve to easily identify it.   Holotype.  ♀.  Kauaʻi:  Nā Pali-Kona F[orest] R[eserve], Alakaʻi Wilderness Preserve, NE of Wai[a]koali Bog 1; 1260 m; 22.1300, -159.5774; 12–13 vii 2023; K. A. Austin; LED bucket trap / DNA extraction KA0859 / KAA diss. #1084 (UHIM).  Paratypes  (15♀♀).  Kauaʻi:  1♀, same as holotype / KAA diss. #1405 (UHIM). 1♀, Nā Pali-Kona F[orest] R[eserve], Alakaʻi Wilderness Preserve, Wai[a]koali Bog 1; 1265 m; 22.1297, -159.5780; 13–14 vii 2023; K. A. Austin; LED bucket trap / DNA extraction KA0860 / KAA diss. #1085 (UHIM). 2♀♀, Nā Pali-Kona Forest Reserve, Alakaʻi Swamp Trail; 3800 ft [1158 m]; 26 vii 1982 / K. & E. Sattler (NHMUK). 1♀, Nā Pali-Kona Forest Reserve, Pihea Trail; 4200 ft [1280 m]; 14 ix 1973 / K. & E. Sattler (NHMUK). 5♀♀, Waiʻalae Valley (Waiʻalae Cabin); 3600 ft [1097 m]; 7 x 1973 / K. & E. Sattler (NHMUK). 1♀, same as previous except 6 x 1973 (NHMUK). 1♀, Kōkeʻe State Park, Awaʻawapuhi Valley; 4000 ft [1219 m]; 15 ix 1973 / K. & E. Sattler (NHMUK). 1♀, Kōkeʻe State Park, Honopu Trail; 4000 ft [1219 m]; 22 viii 1973 / K. & E. Sattler (NHMUK). 1♀, Kōkeʻe State Park, Halemanu; 3400 ft [1036 m]; 23 viii 1973 / K. & E. Sattler (NHMUK). 1♀, Kōkeʻe State Park, Kauaikinanā (Mohihi Road); 3500 ft [1067 m]; 25 ix 1973 / K. & E. Sattler (NHMUK).   Description. Female (n=16).    Head.  Scaling on ventral half of frons short, tightly appressed, pale yellow; scaling on dorsal half long, shaggy, golden brown; scaling on vertex pale golden brown; scaling on lateral surface of labial palpus glossy brown, paler dorsally; scaling on medial surface pale yellow; scape brown or golden brown, rarely straw yellow; dorsal scales of flagellum with alternating rows of dark brown and golden yellow or golden brown scales.  Thorax.  Scales on pronotum golden yellow, a small patch of black or gray scales present along posterior margin, rarely with mahogany red scales intermixed; tegulae golden yellow. Foreleg with lateral scaling brown or dark brown; midleg with femur, tibia pale brown, tarsi brown or dark brown, segments ringed with pale yellow scales; hindlegs pale yellow or gray, tarsi gray, segments ringed with pale yellow scales. Dorsal surface of FW (FWL: 5.2–5.7 mm; FAR: ca. 2.6–2.8, Figs 4G, H) with base of costa pale mahogany red, small black costal strigulae present, basal third of FW pale yellow or golden brown, with scattered gray scales below cell; an oblique line running from ca. 0.3× costa to ca. 0.5× inner margin, becoming faint, directed to tornus below cell; distal two-thirds of FW with ground color dark gray or black, heavily overlaid with mahogany red scales, faint golden yellow strigulae often apparent along costa; fringe pale reddish-orange, with longer pale yellow and dark gray scales at tornus. Dorsal surface of HW gray, paler basally; fringe gray. Ventral surface of FW dark gray, iridescent purple when viewed obliquely under magnification, pale golden yellow strigulae present along costa. Ventral surface of HW similar to dorsal surface, gray, with faint iridescent purple scales similar to ventral surface of FW.  Abdomen.  Vestiture of abdomen with scaling silver or gray, paler ventrally. Genitalia (Fig. 6B) with papillae anales slender, apically acute, approximate, but separate, evenly roughened on ventral surface; apophyses anteriores and posteriores both ca. 0.7× S7, straight, flattened distally; sterigma thin, simple, shallow; ostium narrow, simple; colliculum present, ring-like, weakly developed, open on ventral face, broader than long; ductus bursae ca. 0.6× S7, straight, slender; corpus burse small, elliptical, entirely membranous; signum absent; ductus seminalis arising near base of ductus bursae, similar in length and width; bulla seminalis equal in size and shape to corpus bursae.   Comments.  Surprisingly, no males are known of  N .  kupenuia . A moth similar to  N .  kupenuia  has been photographed by Julien Ueda on Kauaʻi and uploaded to iNaturalist [link]. It may be the male of  N .  kupenuia , but would require a voucher specimen in order to confirm.   Etymology.  From Hawaiian “kūpenu ʻia,” meaning “dipped in dye,” referring to the distinctive forewing pattern of this species, which looks like it has been partially dipped in dye.   Biology.  The biology of  N .  kupenuia  is unknown. We hypothesize that it feeds on the stems and shoots of native Urticaceae, like some other species of  Nomewaimea . However, we have collected it in one locality without any observed Urticaceae, so other host plants may be possible.   Distribution.   Nomewaimea kupenuia  is known from several high elevation (ca. 1000–1300 m) locations on Kauaʻi in both mesic and wet forest.   Conservation status.  The conservation status of  N .  kupenuia  is unknown. Determining its host plant(s) would greatly advance its conservation prospects. Klaus and Edith Sattler were the first to collect this species, suggesting that it was rare even at the time of Perkins’ and Kusche’s collecting.
